name: Physics
description: Forces, electricity, light, and the laws of the universe

questions.q: Approximately how long does sunlight take to reach Earth?

source.notes: ~8 minutes 20 seconds. Light travels ~300,000 km/s.

questions.q: In what year did Albert Einstein win the Nobel Prize in Physics?

source.notes: For his explanation of the photoelectric effect, not relativity.

questions.q: Isaac Newton is most famous for formulating which law after watching an apple fall?

source.notes: Universal Law of Gravitation. Published 1687 in Principia Mathematica.

questions.q: What unit is used to measure electric current?

source.notes: Named after French physicist André-Marie Ampère. Symbol: A.

questions.q: The unit 'watt' is a measure of what?

source.notes: 1 watt = 1 joule/second. Named after James Watt (of steam engine fame).

questions.q: What unit measures electrical resistance?

source.notes: Named after Georg Ohm. Symbol: Ω.

questions.q: The newton is the SI unit of what?

source.notes: Force = Mass × Acceleration. 1 newton ≈ the weight of an apple.

questions.q: When light passes from one medium to another and bends, what is this called?

source.notes: Described by Snell's Law. Makes a straw appear bent in water.

questions.q: What type of lens is used in a simple magnifying glass?

source.notes: Converging lens. Thicker in the middle than at the edges.

questions.q: What happens to an object's weight (not mass) when it's on the Moon?

source.notes: About 1/6 as much. Moon's gravity is 1.62 m/s² vs Earth's 9.81 m/s².

questions.q: What is the speed of light in a vacuum?

source.notes: 299,792,458 m/s exactly. Symbol 'c.' The universal speed limit.

questions.q: What is Einstein's famous equation relating mass and energy?

source.notes: Published 1905 as part of special relativity.

questions.q: Which physicist formulated the three laws of motion?

source.notes: 1687. Laws of inertia, F=ma, and action-reaction.

questions.q: What is the name of the theoretical subatomic particle discovered at CERN in 2012?

source.notes: Sometimes called the 'God Particle.' Gives mass to other elementary particles.

questions.q: What kind of circuit has components connected in a single path?

source.notes: Current flows through each component in sequence. If one breaks, all break.

questions.q: Which instrument is used to measure electric current?

source.notes: Connected in series. Measures current in amperes. A voltmeter (parallel) measures voltage.

questions.q: What phenomenon causes a metal to shorten when cooled and lengthen when heated?

source.notes: Why bridges have expansion joints. Liquid mercury thermometers rely on this.

questions.q: What is the smallest unit of matter that retains the properties of an element?

source.notes: From Greek 'atomos' — 'indivisible.' Though we've since split the atom.

questions.q: The Doppler effect describes a change in what, as a source moves toward or away from an observer?

source.notes: Explains the pitch change of a passing ambulance siren. Used in astronomy to measure cosmic expansion.

questions.q: What is the SI unit of frequency?

source.notes: Symbol: Hz. One cycle per second. Named after Heinrich Hertz.
